sections.title: Workflows: from $.state strings to typed handles
sections.intro: Today a Blok workflow is a flat array of step objects, and every cross-step data reference is a hand-typed string the compiler never sees: `"$.state.validate.productId"` for inputs, `"js/\`...${ctx.state...}\`"` for interpolation, and a raw `ctx.*` JS string for branch conditions. A typo, a renamed output field, or a wrong alias all fail silently at runtime. The new handle syntax replaces every string with a typed value: `step(id, node, inputs)` returns a handle shaped like the node's Zod output, references are real property reads, `tpl` keeps interpolation structural, and `branch` takes a boolean handle (or a `gt`/`eq` op) instead of a raw string. Same auto-persistence, same if-else engine underneath — just a surface the type system can actually check.

blocks.label: A multi-step workflow that fetches data and chains it forward
blocks.oldCaption: $.state.<id> strings and js/ interpolation — the step id and every reference to it can silently drift apart
blocks.newCaption: Typed handles: step(id, node, inputs) returns the node's output shape; references are real property reads — autocompleted and refactor-safe

blocks.label: The same workflow with a branch on a fetched value (the raw-ctx footgun)
blocks.oldCaption: branch.when is a raw JS string the if-else node evals against ctx — wrong alias or a $ proxy here silently mis-evaluates; the eq() escape hatch is still an untyped string builder
blocks.newCaption: branch takes a boolean handle (or a typed gt/eq op over handle fields) — the condition is the node's actual output, type-checked, with no raw ctx string anywhere

takeaways: One orchestration language stays: workflows are TypeScript. The 7 runtimes are for nodes (the work units), not orchestration — moving to all-.ts authoring loses nothing.

step("id", node, inputs) returns a typed handle shaped like the node's Zod output. Reading validate.productId records a structural { $ref: { step, path } } — a real property access, autocompleted and refactor-safe, not a magic string.

Every step's output is already auto-persisted to state[id] on success. The handle is just the typed surface over that — referenceable anywhere downstream, no manual save step. ephemeral:true still opts side-effect steps out.

branch takes a boolean handle (stock.inStock) or a typed op (gt(stock.available, validate.qty)). Today's escape hatch — the eq()/gt() comparators — still hand you an untyped string builder over a $ proxy; the new ops read real handle fields, so a renamed output fails to compile. tpl does the same for string interpolation.

No $, no js/, no ctx anywhere in authoring. ctx.state / ctx.prev / ctx.request become internal runtime plumbing; the mapper resolves the same { $ref } against state[step] using today's engine — cleaner surface, identical runtime.

sections.title: Nodes + the death of Nodes.ts
sections.intro: The node itself was never the problem — `defineNode()` (Zod in, Zod out, a pure `execute`) stays byte-for-byte the same. What changes is everything *around* it: today a hand-maintained `Nodes.ts` registry maps magic strings like `"@blokjs/api-call"` to imported nodes — typed `Record<string, BlokService<unknown>>`, where the `unknown` erases every output type — and workflows reference nodes by re-typing that same string (`use: "@blokjs/api-call"`) with zero type-checking. In the new model that registry file is deleted: a local TS node is imported straight into the workflow and passed to `step()` by value, so the node's Zod output type flows directly into the handle. Cross-runtime nodes (Go/Python/…) keep the language-neutral `{ use, type:"runtime.x" }` IR, but it's now emitted from a generated typed stub instead of a string an author guessed.

blocks.label: The node definition — unchanged across old and new
blocks.oldCaption: defineNode() — Zod in, Zod out, pure execute. Nothing here was ever broken.
blocks.newCaption: defineNode() is untouched — same Zod schemas, same execute. Only the import package consolidates (@blokjs/runner → @blokjs/core).

blocks.label: The registry — a hand-maintained file vs. a direct import
blocks.oldCaption: Every node double-registered: once in the map, again as a string in each workflow — all typed `unknown`.
blocks.newCaption: Import the node, pass it to step() by value. The registry file is gone; the node's output type flows into the handle and any handle is referenceable downstream.

blocks.label: Cross-runtime nodes — the one string that survives, now generated
blocks.oldCaption: Out-of-process nodes are pure strings — name, runtime kind, and output shape all unchecked until the run.
blocks.newCaption: A generated typed stub gives Go/Python nodes the same compile-time handle as local TS — the runtime string is emitted, never hand-written.

takeaways: `defineNode()` does not change — Zod input/output and a pure `execute` were never the friction. The only edit to a node file is the package the import comes from (`@blokjs/runner` → `@blokjs/core`), which belongs to the packaging dimension. The node-internal `ctx.response.data` fallback stays — that's runtime plumbing inside a node, not the author-facing surface the new model replaces.

`triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts` is deleted. Today it's a hand-maintained `Record<string, BlokService<unknown>>` that erases every node's output type and forces a second edit (plus a magic string in every workflow) per node. A local TS node is now imported straight into the workflow and passed to `step()` by value.

The `use:` magic string disappears for local nodes. `step("id", apiCall, {...})` carries the node's Zod output type into the handle, so `stock.inStock` is checked at compile time and a deleted/renamed node is a red squiggle before save — not a `"Node type X not found"` at run time.

Auto-persistence is what makes the handle work anywhere: every step's output lands in `state[id]` on success, so a handle declared outside a branch (like `stock`) is referenceable inside it with no manual save step. The handle is just the typed surface over Blok's existing default-store.

Cross-runtime nodes (Go/Rust/Python/…) keep the language-neutral `{ use, type:"runtime.x" }` IR — the lingua franca the canvas and JSON twin consume — but it's now emitted from a generated typed stub (`runtimeNode<In,Out>(...)`) produced from the node's manifest, the same codegen that already writes `register_user_nodes.go`. A Python node becomes fully typed on the TS authoring side. One mental model for every node: import it, hand it to `step()`, read its handle — whether the work runs in-process in TS or out-of-process in Python is an IR detail, not an authoring concern.

sections.title: The unified @blokjs/core package
sections.intro: Today a Blok project wires together ~10 separately-versioned `@blokjs/*` packages, all locked to the same monorepo version, plus a hand-maintained `Nodes.ts` registry that `blokctl install` regex-patches in place. The two most-imported packages are named `@blokjs/helper` (the DSL) and `@blokjs/helpers` (a bag of nodes) — a one-letter trap that bites everyone. The redesign collapses the framework into ONE package: `import { workflow, step, branch, defineNode, http } from "@blokjs/core"`. Nodes become ordinary npm packages you import directly (no `Nodes.ts` registry), and triggers like `http` are opt-in submodules — so the dependency list shrinks from a lockstep cluster to a single framework dep plus exactly the nodes you actually use.

blocks.label: The imports + node registry a scaffolded project carries today vs. one framework import
blocks.oldCaption: ~10 @blokjs/* packages, a hand-patched Nodes.ts map, the helper/helpers trap, and three stringly-typed ref dialects ($. / js/ / raw-ctx when)
blocks.newCaption: One import from @blokjs/core; nodes imported directly; one typed-handle surface replaces $., js/, raw-ctx when, and Nodes.ts

blocks.label: The project's package.json dependency list — lockstep cluster vs. one framework dep + the nodes you use
blocks.oldCaption: 10 lockstep-versioned deps; helper vs helpers side by side; framework and nodes can't move independently
blocks.newCaption: One framework dep + only the node packages you use; triggers opt-in; nodes on independent semver

takeaways: One import replaces ~10: `@blokjs/core` folds today's `@blokjs/runner` + `@blokjs/shared` + `@blokjs/helper` (the DSL) into a single versioned framework package, so `import { workflow, step, branch, defineNode, http } from "@blokjs/core"` is the whole framework surface — nodes stay separate packages.

The `@blokjs/helper` (DSL) vs `@blokjs/helpers` (nodes bag) one-letter trap is gone — the DSL is part of core, and nodes are explicit, directly-imported packages, not a `HELPER_NODES` grab-bag spread into a registry map.

`Nodes.ts` is deleted: local TS nodes are imported straight into the workflow (the import IS the registration), and cross-runtime nodes get a generated typed stub — no hand-patched `Record<string, BlokService>` that `blokctl install` regex-edits and silently drops a node from.

Three stringly-typed ref dialects collapse to one: today authors juggle `$.state.<id>` for reads, `js/\`...\`` to interpolate a ref into a string, and a RAW `ctx.*` expression for `branch` `when` (a `$.`-prefixed `when` compiles fine but 500s at runtime). Handles plus `tpl`/`gt` give one typed surface with no `$`, `js/`, or `ctx` anywhere.

Lockstep release ends: today every `@blokjs/*` dep pins the same monorepo version and each node re-declares runner/shared as its own deps, so a runner patch forces a coordinated re-release of everything. With core as one dep and nodes on independent semver — behind deprecated re-export shims for the old names — the framework and each node package move on their own.

sections.title: Context & passing data between steps
sections.intro: Today a Blok author has to learn the runtime's plumbing to move data between steps: five overlapping ctx surfaces (ctx.state / ctx.prev / ctx.vars / ctx.response / ctx.request), a $ proxy that secretly compiles to js/ctx.* strings, node outputs that arrive wrapped in a .data envelope, and a branch condition that is a raw ctx string the typechecker never sees. The new model exposes exactly two things to authors — typed step handles and the typed req — and the entire ctx.* surface becomes invisible runtime plumbing. Auto-persistence is unchanged underneath, so reading a far-upstream step is the same one-liner as reading the adjacent one.

blocks.label: A real multi-step workflow that threads request data, an adjacent output, and a far-upstream output — then branches on it
blocks.oldCaption: Five ctx surfaces, $ that hides js/ strings, a .data envelope to unwrap, and a raw-string branch the compiler can't check
blocks.newCaption: Two surfaces only — typed handles + req; no envelope, no id strings, far-upstream reads are one-liners

blocks.label: Every old ctx.* / $ / js/ form and the single handle form that replaces it
blocks.oldCode:
// ---- THE OLD AUTHORING SURFACE (everything below is something an author had to learn) ----

ctx.request.body            // trigger payload (also aliased ctx.req.body)
ctx.req.params.id           // same object, second name
$.req.query.page            // $ proxy -> compiles to "js/ctx.req.query.page" verbatim
                            //   (the proxy does NOT rewrite req->request; it emits what you type)

ctx.prev.data               // the IMMEDIATELY previous step's output envelope
ctx.response.data           // same object as ctx.prev, third name
$.prev.data                 // -> "js/ctx.prev.data"

ctx.state.validate.productId      // a NAMED step's output, by id string
ctx.vars.validate.productId       // same object as ctx.state, alias
$.state.validate.productId        // -> "js/ctx.state.validate.productId"
"js/ctx.state.checkStock.data"    // the hand-written wire string the $ proxy emits

branch({ when: "ctx.state.checkStock.data.inStock === true" })  // RAW ctx string, no $
$.error.message             // captured error inside tryCatch.catch -> "js/ctx.error.message"
blocks.oldCaption: Three names for the same object, two ways to write each, one of them an unchecked string
blocks.newCaption: One typed handle collapses state/vars/prev/response into the same thing; req covers request

takeaways: The old surface exposed the runtime's plumbing: ctx.state and ctx.vars are the same object, ctx.prev and ctx.response are the same object, and the $ proxy is just typed sugar that deep-walks to js/ctx.* strings at definition time (unwrapProxies in core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts) — an author had to learn three names for one thing and two ways to write each. The proxy also emits what you type verbatim ($.req -> js/ctx.req), so the aliases leak into the wire strings too.

ctx.prev only holds the IMMEDIATELY previous step and is overwritten every step, so any non-adjacent read forced authors into ctx.state.<id> with a step-id string they had to keep in sync by hand — and to remember each node's output sits under a .data envelope. A handle is shaped like the node's Zod output (no .data) and makes far-upstream reads identical to adjacent ones: you just reference the const.

The worst footgun is branch when: it is a RAW ctx string evaluated directly against ctx, never mapper-resolved and never typechecked (the shipped workflows/json/countries-vs-facts.json uses when: 'ctx.request.query.countries === "true"'). Write $.state.x there and it compiles to a js/ctx string the condition evaluator ignores, silently evaluating to undefined. eq/gt/lt (already in @blokjs/helper) replace it with a compiler-checked typed op.

Nothing changes underneath: every step's output is still auto-persisted to state[id] on success, and the mapper still resolves a structural reference against state[step] before the consuming step runs — same engine. The handle is just the typed, named surface over that existing auto-persistence: no manual save step, and ephemeral:true still opts a side-effect-only step out.

Net author-visible surface drops from {ctx.request, ctx.req, ctx.prev, ctx.response, ctx.state, ctx.vars, ctx.error, $, js/ strings, .data envelopes, raw when} to exactly {step handles, req, tpl, eq/gt/lt, forEach}. No $, no js/, no ctx anywhere in authoring.

sections.title: Persistence: save every response, use it anywhere
sections.intro: The founder question is "can I save any node's response as a variable and use it at any point later?" Blok already answers yes at the engine level: every step's output is auto-persisted to state[id] on success (PersistenceHelper.applyStepOutput, Rule 3), and that slot is referenceable anywhere downstream — not just the adjacent step. The redesign keeps that exact default and exact mechanic: a { $ref } resolved against state[step] by the same Mapper before the consuming step runs. What changes is only the surface — instead of the stringly-typed "$.state.<id>" you hold a typed handle returned by step(), shaped like the node's Zod output. Same persistence, same resolution engine, a surface the compiler can check.

blocks.label: Save a node response once, read it 4 steps later (the founder question, answered)
blocks.oldCaption: Auto-persist already lets any step read any earlier step — but every reference is an unchecked string
blocks.newCaption: Same auto-persist, same {$ref}→state[id] resolution — now every cross-step reference is a typed handle the compiler checks

blocks.label: ephemeral opt-out + the persistence knobs (as / spread) carry over unchanged
blocks.oldCaption: ephemeral / spread / as are per-step string flags on an untyped step object
blocks.newCaption: Same Rule 0–3 engine; ephemeral / spread / as move to a typed options bag, no behavior change

takeaways: The founder ask — 'save any node response as a variable, usable at any point' — is already how Blok works: applyStepOutput auto-persists every step's output to state[id] on success (Rule 3). Errored steps write nothing (Rule 0), so state[id] === undefined is a truthful 'did it succeed?' check. The redesign keeps this default verbatim; there is no manual save step in either model.

'Use it anywhere' is literal, not adjacent-only. ctx.prev is just the previous step; cross-step access goes through state[id] — which the new createOrder step demonstrates by reaching a handle 3 steps back, alongside two intermediate ones.

The mechanic is unchanged: reading a handle (validate.productId) records { $ref: { step, path } }; the same Mapper that resolves '$.state.*' today resolves that ref against state[step] before the consuming step runs. New surface, identical resolution engine.

Only the surface changes — stringly-typed '$.state.<id>' (and mid-string 'js/`...`' templates, now tpl) become typed handles shaped like the node's Zod output. A wrong step id or field path is a compile error instead of a runtime MapperResolutionError.

The persistence knobs carry over one-for-one: ephemeral opts a side-effect step out of state, spread flattens a multi-output node's keys to the top level (so its handle exposes load.user / load.profile, not load), as renames the slot, as/spread stay mutually exclusive. They move from per-step string flags to a typed options bag with no behavior change.

Why keep auto-persist-all rather than opt-in saves: opt-in would reintroduce the manual 'save this' step the founder is trying to escape, and would make the truthful-state contract (Rule 0) unreliable. Auto-persist-all + typed handles gives free cross-step access AND compile-time safety — the alternatives trade one away.

sections.title: Cross-runtime nodes: from stringly-typed runtime refs to generated typed stubs
sections.intro: Blok's superpower is that a node can run in any of 7 runtimes (Go, Rust, Java, C#, PHP, Ruby, Python) over one gRPC contract (`blok.runtime.v1`). But authoring a workflow against a Python node today means hand-writing a magic pair — `use: "risk-score"` + `type: "runtime.python3"` — registering the local TS nodes through a `Nodes.ts` map, and trusting that the field names you pass match a schema written in another language. There is zero type-safety across the boundary. The new model generates a typed stub from each node's JSON Schema — the very `input_schema_json` / `output_schema_json` the runtime already returns from its `ListNodes` RPC (`NodeDescriptor` in runtime.proto) — so a Python node is imported and called exactly like a local TS node: same handle, same autocomplete, same IR. `defineNode()` in each SDK and the gRPC wire contract are untouched — only the authoring surface changes.

blocks.label: Calling a Python node from a TS workflow — stringly-typed runtime ref + Nodes.ts map
blocks.oldCaption: `use` + `type: "runtime.python3"` are opaque strings; Nodes.ts is a hand-kept map; nothing type-checks across the language boundary
blocks.newCaption: the Python node is a generated typed stub, imported and called exactly like a local node — full autocomplete and compile-time checks across the language boundary

blocks.label: Per-runtime registration — codegen shim (compiled) vs fs-scan (dynamic), now uniform
blocks.oldCaption: two divergent registration paths — codegen shim that recompiles every dev-spawn (Go/Rust/Java/C#) vs fs-scan with no type surface (Python/Ruby/PHP) — and no node schema ever reaches the TS author
blocks.newCaption: one `blokctl nodes sync` reads each runtime's existing ListNodes manifest and emits a typed stub — identical for all 7 runtimes; the IR and gRPC dispatch are unchanged

takeaways: The runtime ref is no longer a string. `use: "risk-score"` + `type: "runtime.python3"` becomes a generated, typed `runtimeNode<In, Out>` stub, imported and called like any local TS node — a wrong field name or stale schema is a compile error, not a 3am gRPC failure.

Nodes.ts is deleted. Today's `triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts` (`Record<string, BlokService<unknown>>`) is replaced by plain imports for local TS nodes and generated stubs for cross-runtime nodes. There is no hand-maintained node map to drift out of sync.

Registration is uniform across all 7 runtimes. The old split — codegen shim recompiled every dev-spawn (Go/Rust/Java/C#, e.g. `register_user_nodes.go`) vs fs-scan with no type surface (Python/Ruby/PHP via `BLOK_NODES_DIR`) — collapses into one `blokctl nodes sync` that reads each runtime's existing `ListNodes` manifest.

Nothing on the wire changes. `defineNode()` in each SDK and the `blok.runtime.v1` gRPC contract (Execute / ListNodes / NodeDescriptor with `input_schema_json` / `output_schema_json`) are untouched. The stub is pure compile-time ergonomics over the schema the runtime already publishes.

Same IR, same engine. `step(...)` against a Python node compiles to the identical language-neutral `{ use, type: "runtime.python3", inputs: { $ref } }` IR, resolved by the same mapper and dispatched by the same `GrpcRuntimeAdapter` as today.

frame.headline: Blok core stays one TypeScript orchestration language but trades every stringly-typed surface — $.state refs, js/ templates, raw ctx branch conditions, the Nodes.ts registry, and ~10 lockstep packages — for typed step handles over the unchanged auto-persistence engine, shipped as one @blokjs/core package.
overview: Authoring becomes typed handles: step("id", node, inputs) returns a value shaped like the node's Zod output; reading a field records a structural { $ref:{step,path} }. No $, no js/, no ctx anywhere an author types — those become internal runtime plumbing.

The engine does not move. Auto-persist-every-output-to-state[id]-on-success (PersistenceHelper.applyStepOutput, Rules 0/2/3 verified) and the Mapper that resolves refs against state[step] are byte-identical; the handle is just the typed surface over them. Identical runtime, checkable surface.

defineNode() is untouched and Nodes.ts is deleted: local TS nodes are imported straight into the workflow (the import IS the registration, carrying the Zod output type into the handle); cross-runtime nodes get a generated runtimeNode<In,Out> stub from the runtime's ListNodes manifest (input_schema_json/output_schema_json, verified in runtime.proto). One mental model for all 7 runtimes.

Branch conditions and string interpolation stop being untyped: branch takes a boolean handle or a typed op, tpl keeps refs structural inside strings. Today's eq/gt/lt are confirmed string builders (return type `string`) — the new ops read real handle fields, so a renamed output fails to compile instead of silently evaluating to undefined (the verified countries-vs-facts.json raw-when footgun).

Packaging collapses ~10 lockstep @blokjs/* deps into one @blokjs/core (runner + shared + helper DSL), killing the @blokjs/helper-vs-helpers one-letter trap. Nodes stay independent semver packages; triggers like http are opt-in submodules.

The IR is the lingua franca: the .ts DSL compiles to language-neutral { use, type:"runtime.x", inputs:{$ref} } JSON that the canvas, JSON twin, and AI all consume — same IR and same GrpcRuntimeAdapter dispatch as today, whether the work runs in-process in TS or out-of-process in Python.
Net author-visible surface shrinks from {ctx.request/req/prev/response/state/vars/error, $, js/ strings, .data envelopes, raw when} to exactly {step handles, req, tpl, eq/gt/lt, forEach}.
frame.persistenceVerdict: Yes — keep auto-persisting every node's response as a usable variable, referenceable anywhere downstream, not just adjacent. This is already exactly how Blok works (applyStepOutput stores every successful step to state[id]; errored steps write nothing, making state[id]===undefined a truthful success check), so the founder's ask is satisfied with zero engine change — the redesign only adds a typed handle surface over it. The alternative (opt-in saves) is strictly worse: it reintroduces the manual \"save this\" step the founder is trying to escape AND breaks the truthful-state contract, while buying nothing, since auto-persist-all + typed handles already gives both free cross-step access and compile-time safety. ephemeral:true remains the deliberate opt-out for side-effect-only steps, and as/spread stay as advanced rename/flatten knobs.
migration: Hybrid by construction: the new DSL and the old v1/v2 array-of-steps shape both compile to the same JSON IR, so a project can hold handle-style and string-style workflows side by side and run them on one runner — no big-bang rewrite.

Back-compat shims: old @blokjs/runner / @blokjs/shared / @blokjs/helper names stay as deprecated re-exports from @blokjs/core, so existing imports keep resolving; the only forced edit in a node file is the import source.

Nodes.ts removal is mechanical: a codemod walks each workflow's `use:` strings, resolves them against the old registry map, rewrites local refs to direct imports + step(node,...) calls, and replaces runtime refs (use+type pair) with generated runtimeNode stubs from `blokctl nodes sync` (reads each runtime's existing ListNodes manifest).

Ref codemod: `$.state.<id>` / `js/\`...\`` / raw-ctx `when` strings rewrite to handle reads / tpl / eq-gt-lt ops; unresolvable expressions (dynamic paths) are left as-is with a `// ponytail: hand-migrate` marker rather than guessed.

What breaks (intentionally, and only at the typed surface): a wrong step id, deleted node, or renamed output field that used to fail at runtime now fails at compile time — louder, earlier, before save. Runtime behavior of correct workflows is unchanged.

Lockstep release ends post-migration: framework ships as one @blokjs/core version and each node moves on independent semver, so a runner patch no longer forces a coordinated re-release of every node package.
consistencyNotes: eq/gt/lt are described in two roles across sections (today's "escape hatch" vs the "new typed ops"). Source confirms today's versions return plain `string` over the $ proxy — so they genuinely change, not just get reused. Resolution: the framing states the new ops read real handle fields (compile-checked) where today's return an untyped string builder; both claims hold and are not contradictory.

The persistence and context sections both assert "nothing changes underneath." Verified true against applyStepOutput and the Mapper — the only divergence is surface, so I kept a single consistent statement ("identical runtime, checkable surface") rather than letting each section imply a different degree of change.

The runtimeNode stub is sourced from the node manifest. Verified the manifest is the ListNodes RPC's input_schema_json/output_schema_json in runtime.proto — consistent across the nodes and cross-runtime sections; no drift between "manifest" and "JSON Schema" wording (they are the same bytes).

ephemeral/as/spread are described as carried-over knobs in both persistence and context sections; applyStepOutput confirms all three still branch there. Consistent — they move from string flags to a typed options bag with no behavior change.
